interactions.summary: PBD asks Jiang to compare his life in Canada, the United States, and China; Jiang says he was born in China, raised in Toronto, attended Yale, and contrasts Canada’s hockey dream, America’s entrepreneurship dream, and China’s bureaucratic dream.

PBD asks why Jiang returned to China and asks him to explain the PBS documentary incident; Jiang begins by recounting his English teaching, journalism, PBS contract, worker protest filming, detention, confession, and deportation without charges.

speaker_notes.note: SPEAKER_01 is Patrick Bet-David or the PBD side of the interview; SPEAKER_00 is Jiang except for brief intro/audio fragments. Diarization is treated as a hint and the exchange context controls attribution.

claims.claim: Jiang compresses three national dreams as Canada wanting to be hockey players, America wanting to be entrepreneurs, and China wanting to be bureaucrats.
Jiang says he was detained and deported from China after filming worker protests for a PBS documentary on China’s WTO entry while lacking a journalist visa.

signature_moments.moment: America’s dream is independence from government control: start a business, make it, and speak your mind.
signature_moments.source_phrase: be independent and be free of government control
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It establishes the positive American ideal Jiang later contrasts with censorship and bureaucracy.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: Jiang’s shorthand comparison of Canada as hockey, America as entrepreneurship, and China as bureaucracy.

chronology_notes.note: Jiang says he was born in China, moved to Toronto at age six, attended Yale for four years, and has been in China since 1999.
